Back in 2008-ish, Google conducted a large prediction market experiment which I estimate costed them between $50k and $15M in implied wages (point estimate of $1.5M). 1/3

I think that the fact that they dropped them afterwards tells us something important about prediction markets not being worth it, but some of my collaborators disagree

Ideally, twitter users would each have an easily accessible RSS feed, and I could plug that in to an aggregation algorithm of my choice, 2/n

For instance, maybe I don't want to see users weighted by how often they twit, but rather by estimates of how insightful and novel their tweets are, and an ML algorithm could try to approximate that. 3/n

Consider also that this option is private by default. It also has no ads. Things like https://t.co/s9Jezhpzp9 have no effect. The last example is ambiguously good, but illustrates that this method gives me, rather than twitter, more power 4/n
Also, I now got the hang out of the n/n thing. 5/5

Wrote half a paper with the title "Amplify Rootclaim: Probably a Better Method for Forecasting Unanswerable Questions" last night between 03:00 and 05:30. Curious to see now that I'm awake if it is any good/how good it is. 1/n
I remember feeling fairly insightful while writing, but I'm going to guess it's between decent to pretty ok. 2/n
Eh, reading it now I'd say it's fairly decent to actually pretty good. I left a lot of puns/zingers and made some glaring arithmetic errors.
Update: Arithmetic errors caused by being sleepy now, not insomniac before.
Would do again 5/5

Should EAs earn to give? I just posted a model of spending and movement building w/ Phil Trammel which tries to address this.
https://t.co/uIRTfSd8MP
Betteridge's law of headlines would seem to apply, at least in the limit, unless you are really good at making money.
But we don't know whether we are in the limit already because we are missing empirical data. Still, we suspect.
Here is the skeleton of the model. All the integrals have been exiled to the latex article, so the EA Forum post should be pretty readable. https://t.co/gLQE9tUuZZ

I'd say that most of the impact goes to Phil, because although I put in most of the time in hours, I spent many of them exploring dead-ends + he's the much better economist, has paid the fixed costs of academia, and did the final push.

Hey @jtmonrad, @PTetlock, @EzraKarger if I'm understanding your paper correctly https://t.co/71FvKW4gfI, the group of interest in the first study looks like the first diagram, but the second study looks like the second diagram? https://t.co/Rsi6K1Gqvi

@jtmonrad @PTetlock @EzraKarger I.e., in the first study survey participants try to predict excellent forecasters (incentivized by 1/distance), and excellent forecasters try to predict reality (incentivized by Brier), but in the second study the two groups try to predict each other?

Yeah, I'm re-reading this now, I don't get what is *reciprocal* about the first design, or why you can conclude from the first study (which has a control group) that the methods in the second study (which doesn't have a control group) work. https://t.co/1NXSoDqt0G
Or, why you call both things reciprocal

So going through old Google Docs, I find one titled in "LZ Madcap ideas to reduce R0...", where LZ is @LinchZhang. The document was created in May 13, 2020.
@LinchZhang Comparing it with the CDC's guidance at the time (https://t.co/UKmw8jBFt6), Linch's document strikes me as more reasonable.
The CDC is all like ~"you must conduct an expensive hazard assessment using the hierarchy of controls as a sacrifice to Kaunuzoth"
Linch is like: ~"these sets of measures sound broadly good and not too costly under a variety of reasonable assumptions"
Between having access to Linch's document and the CDC's guidance, I'd prefer Linch's document.
Also, Linch gets pooled testing, and the CDC ~never did.

The "@metaforecast" bot has now received some architectural improvements. This should make it easier to scale to many users at the same time, and to add many different options. Suggestions welcome. https://t.co/mMERhqyvkz

@metaforecast The gist of it is that request are now spun off to a separate processes (in this case "screenshotWorker" in the logs above). This makes it easier to process several requests at the same time, and more expensive kinds of request don't impinge on less expensive kinds.

I define the "is x times as valuable" relationship as follows: "A is 2 (respectively x) times as valuable as B" if I am indifferent between 2 (resp. x) units of B, or one unit of A.

Getting hit in the face twice is x times as valuable as getting hit in the face once. What is x?

@marshallk The other way around! I'm thinking is that improved *execution* is more likely than improved prioritization.
@marshallk In particular, I would like to help funders improve their prioritization (if I have $X to donate, what's the optimal way to do it?)
@marshallk ... but this is difficult to do, both at the level of getting people to listen to you and at the level of having things to say
@marshallk So historically, I've had more luck at using evaluations to change the way that people do things, rather than dropping them completely. That could change in the future, though.

Now that Github is down (https://t.co/kZOoVWzXjy), it might be good to advertise the badass open source alternatives from @giteaio (https://t.co/ions4ZOtFQ, to self-host) and @codeberg_org (https://t.co/Pm39lE6FqH, which itself uses gitea)
@giteaio @codeberg_org Github being down is a big deal because they host a large part of internet infrastructure, and yet are evil
E.g., since being acquired by Microsoft, they have trained a huge machine learning model on their repositories without their owners consent.

They are also pushing for the stupid GitHub Codespaces which traps developers into their ecosystem, and misses the whole point of git being a distributed system. For instance, if you were using Github Codespaces to access your code, now you can't, because Github is down.

Overall, I've been playing with gitea these last few days, and it has been a joy & a bit of an intellectual challenge to set up and customize. But now, e.g., the code for https://t.co/T0vyHpFZs0 is up at https://t.co/oktklU98Pe even though Github is down.
Github is not special or unassailable, it was just the first, but is not the best. Open source 4tw.

We first construct an idealized pandemonium (fig. 1). Each possible pattern of the set, represented by a demon in a box, computes his similarity with the image simultaneously on view to all of them, and gives an output depending monotonically on that similarity. https://t.co/MrT7oTPAA9
Each cognitive demon computes a shriek, and from all the shrieks the highest level demon of all, the decision demon, merely selects the loudest
Feature weighting consists of altering the weights assigned to the subdemons by the cognitive demons so as to maximize the score of the entire y Pandemonium.
We are at present running our Pandemonium on an IBM 704.

In speed and information theory, I really consider that speed in the classic sense of computation is so completely irrelevant to this problem -  the number of binary operations one does ,a second - that it does not Interest me very much though it interests computer designers.

I would rather like to see lots of operations which could conceivably be done in parallel, being done sequentially, because this is the only machine we have, and then I hope people will have machines which will work in parallel,
so that when I want a machine to do twice as difficult a problem I merely build twice as big a machine, instead of letting one machine work twice as long.

I just posted a fairly comprehensive piece on prediction markets inside companies: https://t.co/A8NYnt3ZBJ , written with @eli_lifland and @mishayagudin
@eli_lifland @mishayagudin Probably worth at least skimming for those who seek to have good models of the general area.
We reviewed the academic consensus on and corporate track record of prediction markets. Personally, I wasn't very impressed.

The academic consensus seems to overstate their benefits and promisingness. Lack of good tech, the difficulty of writing good and informative questions, and social disruptiveness are likely to be among the reasons contributing to their failure.
We are much more sure about the fact that prediction markets fail to gain adoption than about any particular explanation of why this is.

In the end, we didn't recommend adopting company-internal prediction markets. We surveyed some neat alternatives, like automatic prediction markets, surveys, Delphi setups, or ML, and we generally think those are preferable.

Internal prediction markets just have fairly high overhead, and select from a much smaller pool of candidates. I.e., the sharks at Polymarket or PredictIt are going to be much better than the sharks at Acme, Inc.

More spicily, I came away fairly ¿full of pity? for the academic literature on the topic. Papers upon papers find that prediction markets inside companies should be great, but this clearly isn't the case. But academics hyped up their results because of terrible incentives. https://t.co/VOiXL2yppu

@dschwarz26 @eli_lifland @mishayagudin Yes, I'm interested, in particular about the steps from good markets to good decisions, and also about whether prediction markets are worth the cost for you on a back of the envelope calculation

@rdeneufville So, like, I don't think that any of the forecasters individually have put 8x the research that you have, but I think that it's plausible that that's the case cumulatively

I am releasing three papers on scoring rules. https://t.co/IILpvQ1u6F https://t.co/JV5xcqdnF0

They are conceptually fairly simple, but I think that they solve some of my past frustrations with improper scoring rules (https://t.co/ZZHyMm19Jy) and with reciprocal scoring (https://t.co/vg9DAmWr8y.)

The first paper proposes some tweaks on prediction markets/scoring rules:

1. Compare forecasters against the house, not against other forecasters
2. Have forecasters risk their own money
3. But have each forecaster participate in their prediction market
Who is the house? The house is the stakeholder who cares about the question.
This scheme incentivizes forecasters to collaborate between each other, which is something that ~nobody else has.

For our friends in less developed jurisdictions, like the US, instead of allowing forecasters to risk their own money, you can promise them a fixed reward in one year, or at question resolution time, and allow them to either bet it or keep it.

The second paper (https://t.co/KsDxaijSuZ) gives an alternative to Ezra Karger et al's reciprocal scoring. Instead of recursive loops, have forecasters predict what a trusted authority will predict. Then, reward that authority in proportion to their probability (or not)
Readers may notice that is scheme is similar to @jlagerros's et. al. https://t.co/mBdF1vBqY1, and to Paul Christiano's ideas more generally.
@jlagerros The third paper gives a pie-in-the-sky method of predicting what a Bayesian updater would predict. Very cool, very fun, but probably harder to implement. https://t.co/F5ZTJpggPe
@jlagerros Thanks to @eli_lifland , @mishayagudin , @g_leech_  for discussion about this paper in particular,
@jlagerros @eli_lifland @mishayagudin @g_leech_ and to @EzraKarger, @Jsevillamol and @ericneyman, @Tegan_McCaslin & others for more general discussion on these topics

One litmus test is whether the participants can screw you over if they are allowed to communicate. In a Keynesian Beauty Contest, or in the Bayesian truth serum setting, it seems like they can. https://t.co/OASnzXKKUJ
Right now, this isn't so bad, because prediction markets/forecasting tournaments are not that widespread. But we want setups that are scalable

And if we create incentive mechanisms that are exploitable by the Polymarket crowd, and then deploy them for something important, the sharks will come and they will exploit the loopholes and take the money
You could also force participants to not communicate with each other, but that is also suboptimal: we know that teams or Delphi-like setups perform better

From the perspective of the forecasting community: If people are distributed from "honorable altruists" to "I will hack the metric", then flawed scoring rules will differentially reward the second group. And I don't like the selection effects this produces.

Some rudimentary thoughts on infinite ethics, written after one too many times encountering someone who drew a blank when encountering infinite expected values.

https://t.co/JO3lVbkJO6 https://t.co/I6VXVuGEyj
tl;dr: You can still rationally choose the best of two options when they both have infinite expected values. But this is not a story the deontologists would tell you.
@EgeErdil2 @keithwynroe So here is a chief case I want to deal with: A is better than B. But the future maybe has infinite value, so A + ∞ = B + ∞. But stochastic dominance can deal with this case.
@EgeErdil2 @keithwynroe Like, not that I disagree with your overall take, but IMHO people give up immediately after encounting infinite expected values, when they could push a bit more
@EgeErdil2 @keithwynroe I agree with the literal meaning, but not with the sentiment. In your particular case, expected values can deal with that just fine.
@EgeErdil2 @keithwynroe And if we can intuitively see that one infinite sequence is better than another one, there isn't any particular reason to think that we couldn't formalize that intuition

Enter CALISTO, a young nobleman who, in the course of his adventures, finds MELIBEA, a young noblewoman, and is bewitched by her appearance.
CALISTO: Your presence, Melibea, exceeds my 99.9% percentile prediction.
MELIBEA: How so, Calisto?

CALISTO: In that the grace of your form, its presentation and concealment, its motions and ornamentation are to me so unforeseen that they make me doubt my eyes, my sanity, and my forecasting prowess.
CALISTO: In that if beauty was an alchemical substance, you would have four parts of it for every one part that all the other dames in the realm together have.
MELIBEA: But do go on Calisto, that your flattery is not altogether unpleasant to my ears.

CALISTO: I must not, for I am in an URGENT MISSION on which the fate of the kingdom rests. And yet, even with my utilitarian inclinations, I would grant you ANY BOON IN MY POWER for A KISS, even if my delay would marginally increase the risk to the realm.
Calisto then climbs up to Melibea's balcony and GETS A KISS. When going down, he LOOKS BACK at Melibea, slips, BREAKS HIS NECK, and DIES.
The END.

A Critical Review of Open Philanthropy’s Bet On Criminal Justice Reform:

From 2013 to 2021, Open Philanthropy donated $200M to criminal justice reform. My best guess is that, from a utilitarian perspective, this was likely suboptimal.

https://t.co/he5z8slWlj

Using Squiggle, a probabilistic programming language, I model the impact of criminal justice systemic reform, some of the best-targetted grants against GiveDirectly and the Against Malaria Foundation.
Systemic reform does not overlap with Against Malaria Foundation. And what might be the best grants in the space are still worse than GiveDirectly in expectation ($5k/~QALY vs $950/QALY).
Here is my estimate for systemic criminal justice reform, in QALYs/$ https://t.co/L4Gj2HQn9T
Here is my estimate for GiveDirectly's impact, in QALYs/$ https://t.co/BeRVEKTUNX

I then give some speculation about why Open Philanthropy did this. There are some sincere hypotheses (this was a reasonable bet that just didn't work, but could have), and some insincere hypotheses (politics corrupting models of the world).

One of the hypothesis I like is that they start with a worldview diversification approach as an approximation of expected value, but then degenerates into a peace pact between the different cause areas.

I then do some updates on OP's speed, rationality, conflict-averseness, etc. TBH, I think that I just started out thinking absurdly highly of OpenPhil. And now I merely just think very/fairly highly of them.
I think that the grant officer realizing and then advocating that their own area was suboptimal would have been heroic. And that just... didn't happen.
I really don't like their moral diversification approach. I think that @albrgr/@PeterFavaloro are absolutely right when they write that:

@albrgr @PeterFavaloro > Overall, having a single “bar” across multiple very different programs and outcome measures is an attractive feature because equalizing marginal returns across different programs is a requirement for optimizing the overall allocation of resources
https://t.co/QnDyNAYJnP

@albrgr @PeterFavaloro and I think that OP should aim for utility maximization across all their programs. Which, tbh, would be really tricky. I think you'd still donate to many things, because of diminishing returns. But I'd also be deeply at peace with everything going to one area.

@albrgr @PeterFavaloro I then also propose fixes: 
1. Have red-teaming contests, such as this one: https://t.co/kYItFyQkcc

@albrgr @PeterFavaloro 2. Allow people to bet large amounts of real $$ agains Open Philanthropy's estimations and assumptions, using the TarasBob method for maximizing predictive accuracy
https://t.co/IkmjKUUWPj
@albrgr @PeterFavaloro I oscillate between thinking “I, a young grasshopper, do not understand”, and “this was clearly suboptimal from the beginning, and obviously so”.

@albrgr @PeterFavaloro Or, more forcefully, I started out being skeptical that a utilitarian, left alone, spontaneously starts exploring criminal justice reform in the US as a cause area, and to some degree I still think that upon further investigation, though I still have significant uncertainty.
@albrgr @PeterFavaloro Link back to the post; I welcome comments: https://t.co/he5z8smuaR

@pablo48pr So anyways, sometimes I find myself doing recently is calculating the expected values of working to prevent unlikely outcomes. So working with probabilities and estimates of values does let you distinguish between work which is less of a priority (super-volcanoes, asteroids)...

@pablo48pr vs work which probably is more of a priority/plausible in a lifetime (solar flares, transformative technological change, ww3 etc.) So idk, I definitely think that you can estimate these meaningfully

@pablo48pr But ultimately, projects to increase civilizational resilience are would probably look valuable either way, so Taleb's point that betting on robustness is also valuable. Not sure how an anti-fragile civilization would look like, though probably less butt-hurt about things.
@pablo48pr Also, I tend to use distributional forecasts with reasonably long tails, rather than point estimates. Not perfect, but probably better than point estimates.

Thread by Domer, one of the sharpest prediction market bettors, on risk of nuclear war. Some thoughts below. https://t.co/OxuJClqXD3

Domah seems to respect prediction markets. And I agree that they tend to have more of an honestly to them. But in case that changes anything, here is us winning ~$18k predicting the Russian invasion of Ukraine... https://t.co/0FKs4kGA1j

; there was a fairly overconfident whale on the other side, and we were following Putin's speeches fairly closely. I imagine that for Domah this amount is close to peanuts, but for us early career people it was a large part of our bankroll.
Prediction markets also tend to suck in your life, which is a reason why we stopped. But anyways, on to the object level...
Note that the forecast was on the 3rd of October. I think that we had more uncertainty back then, and the move of moving probability downwards somewhat rapidly seems *correct*

https://t.co/UidxuMxt5l

> Second group piggybacks their stupid 16% nuke forecast into increased odds of London being nuked.

This doesn't seem like additional evidence. In particular, once Domah establishes to his satisfaction that the forecasts are garbage, sure...
this would be a garbage-in garbage-out situation. But I don't agree with the premise. So no comment.

https://t.co/pR0SaVUr6i

Two of the things that made us—and me personally—go higher are: a) anthropic effects, and b) not *all that much* precedent for Russia specifically fighting against a former Soviet Republic.
I think that point a, anthropics, is a legitimately important consideration that Domah is actually missing. https://t.co/a8EFiVgEvM

A toy model: If you have a machine that gives you either 50% probability of a million or 50% of destroying the world, the only thing which you would *OBSERVE* is the machine repeatedly giving you a million

Similarly, if nukes had been used more throughout history, we might expect the world to look like a more fragile place, and perhaps we wouldn't spend our time fucking around with forecasting. This is easiest to see in the case of full escalation,
but I think it would also apply in cases where there had only been nuclear weapon use. E.g., an India/Pakistan or an India/China nuclear conflict would have had fallout, fucked up suppy chains, etc.
Can we bound this? Arguably. If there are anthropic effects, maybe we would expect a variety of close calls to exist. And there have been some close calls (<https://t.co/Ws0gqzh5CV>)
but it's not clear how close they actually were in practice, i.e., what the ex-ante probability of them escalating was.
The other point is that the historical base rate isn't that informative.

And b) historical frequencies. Domah was saying that nukes haven't been used. And sure, we have somewhat of a track record for that happening (77 years). But we have much fewer years if you are only counting times when a nuclear power was fighting a non-nuclear power
and even fewer years still if we only count Russia vs former Soviet Republics. Here is a dataset that gathers some of these: <https://t.co/bLvSVWbdqI>

@polyminnow Yes, but fewer the more specific you get. Fewer if you  you only count conflicts with border regions (China/Pakistan vs India, Israel vs neighbours), fewer still if you count only the USSR/Russia
Then there are some points about the superiority of prediction markets. Idk man, money is worth less in the event that nukes are used.

Also prediction markets have incentives to not share information & mislead other participants, so they don't feel like the best fit here. Kudos to Polymarket for having the guts to put that market up, but I'd definitely classify that one as pretty experimental
@polyminnow Note that China's nuclear policy is superb, though, <https://t.co/Y62wvqM8M9> https://t.co/FcdK3ZT7tb

Sam also previously said that "there are really compelling reasons to think that the “optimal strategy” to follow is one that probably fails — but if it doesn’t fail, it’s great." https://t.co/Bq3WQv9dkg

The last bet, which seems like it triggered the downfall, was something like trying to position FTX as a champion of crypto regulation. It seems very plausible that this was a high expected value bet, even if it didn't cash out.
I expect that there are lessons to be learnt learnt for future hardcore (expected value, not expected log value) utilitarians, but it's not clear to me what they are yet.

I find it curious that Holden Karnofsky doesn't answer the comments of his blogposts—10 posts since April 7th, 212 comments, 0 replies, his last comment on April 1st. https://t.co/dEeB9NWE0z. 1/10

Compare with Scott Alexander, who does answer the comments, sometimes has roundups of the best comments: <https://t.co/9TXY6Siph0>, and extended back-and-forths <https://t.co/6XQdVEMVeP>, <https://t.co/5SFhb3tkCl> 2/10
So the one gives me more top-down vibes, more like a priest preaching from on high, and the other one gives me more of a "collaboratively seeking the truth" impression. 3/10

Part of this is caused by the fact that the posts are automatically cross-posted to the EA forum from his blog: <https://t.co/06kjLy5Zvh>. But his blog doesn't have comments either. Similar story for LessWrong: https://t.co/QUkFNl1KVf 4/10
There are many other possible explanations, e.g., Karnofsky doesn't seem like an extremely online person, and e.g., I don't think he has a Twitter. He is also probably just very busy. Still. 5/10

This was particularly interesting to me because this other post: <https://t.co/1ZDA9gAhPE> references and contradicts this post from Karnofsky: <https://t.co/rZRW6FF781>, and brings up a fair amount of research and some interesting points in the meantime. 6/10

If this was Scott Alexander, we would already have "Contra Contra Contra Contra Scott Alexander on the track record of futurists". In the process, you can tell a clear story of how Scott Alexander's changes his mind if he posts something wrong online. 7/10

But then why do Karnofsky's blogposts exist? Well, I think having them is better than not having them, and they do give some insight about what the head/co-head of Open Philanthropy is thinking, they can influence people, etc. 8/10

A speculative pet theory of mine is that a primary purpose for the blogposts isn't to influence the general public, but rather to specifically convince Cari Tuna/Dustin Moskovitz that the more speculative side of Open Philanthropy is worth preserving or expanding. 9/10
This would explain why public comments aren't viewed as important. Fin. n/n

Lol, Georgism doesn't work because of Shapley values (<https://t.co/zrDcmL1Vug>). See my comment in the post. I find this hilarious.

Copying my comment there: Land value tax is calculated by approximating the counterfactual value of development as a difference between developed and not-developed land. But this runs into predictable problems arising from the use of counterfactuals (<https://t.co/HRPBHGHEt6>).

You could solve this using Shapley value of development instead. But you don't have access to the Shapley values, because they require multiple counterfactual worlds to calculate, to which you don't have access.
I find this hillarious because I got my start on EA research with the post above, and I like seeing connections between it and other topics of interest.

One question is whether this is fatal. Maybe. Haven't really thought much about this. It's possible that approximating Shapley by counterfactuals works ok most of the time. But then you run into Goodhart's law where capitalistic optimization will find the edge cases.
@g_leech_, @slatestarcodex

Some things the tweet might also mean: The discount rate at which the kid loves the father is equal to the % expansion of the universe.
the kid loves future universes more than the current father
Conditional on the kid loving future universes the same as the current dad, the kid assigns no value to the additional space being created

Technically, the kid didn't specify that he loved the dad as much as the rest of the universe, so either the function is intransitive: dad = universe (which contains dad) ¿>?  dad without the rest of the universe, or the kid assigns 0 value to all that is not dad
This becomes relevant once we notice that "mum" might exist, in which case either the kid loves mum and dad equally and 0 the rest of the universe, or the kid loves dad more than mum
Some kids like these then later grow up to be moral philosopher and a menace to society once their fallacies become more difficult to penetrate.

Another banger in which Duncan:

1. Just writes a wall of text, no images
2. Fits current data to an economic function using his best judgment
3. Tackles a complicated topic.

Note how this is a different brand of Fermi estimate that the ones I do. In particular, I like to tackle the most complicated topic for which I am confident that Fermi estimates are meaningful and useful, so my topics tend to be simpler...
...whereas Duncan tackles a problem of wildly different levels of complexity and gives his best estimate, regardless of the topic

@itaisher @StefanFSchubert @jttiehen @ryancareyai @random_walker @tamaybes I see I was summoned. <https://t.co/mdcJ4R3qAv> might be of interest. At the same time, though, I agree with Itai that this is computationally hard and a-scientific or extra-scientific, you can't get a track record or verify the validity of forecasts for very unusual events.

@itaisher @StefanFSchubert @jttiehen @ryancareyai @random_walker @tamaybes Personally I've oscillated between yeah, we can assign probabilities to everything / no, there is some knightian uncertainty but it's normally overrated / and my current position, which is...

@itaisher @StefanFSchubert @jttiehen @ryancareyai @random_walker @tamaybes ...that long-range forecasts aren't really valid when following in the Tetlock superforecasting tradition but they are totally valid as a matter of normal subjective bayesianism. I'm currently holding the third perspective, mostly.

@itaisher @StefanFSchubert @jttiehen @ryancareyai @random_walker @tamaybes Like, suppose that you are offered the following dilemma: the world avoids all AI risk, but you roll a 1/1000 die and if it falls on 1 the world explodes. Then I roll the die. Imagine that you are offered the same dilemma, but now it's a coinflip. Then I don't take it.

@itaisher @StefanFSchubert @jttiehen @ryancareyai @random_walker @tamaybes That already bounds my probabilities between 0.1% and 50%. And if bisect further, I end around 10% (where I don't really detect the difference between a 10% and an 11%, but I can tell the difference between a 5% or a 20% and a 10%)

@itaisher @StefanFSchubert @jttiehen @ryancareyai @random_walker @tamaybes And I think that the subjective bayesianism approach has something going for it, even if you ultimately think it has flaws—e.g., we can't really argue that much about priors (though we can bet and the one with the better priors will end up winning more often)
@itaisher @StefanFSchubert @jttiehen @ryancareyai @random_walker @tamaybes Anyways, hope that was of help, happy to answer questions

@StefanFSchubert @itaisher @jttiehen @ryancareyai @random_walker @tamaybes The Tetlock superforecasting tradition is a specific line of academic inquiry which started by looking into methods to produce accurate geopolitical forecasts 2-3 years into the future.

@StefanFSchubert @itaisher @jttiehen @ryancareyai @random_walker @tamaybes Subjective Bayesianism is a more general philosophical position which claims, because of Cox's theorem etc., that the optimal epistemology is probabilistic & Bayesian, where the updates and priors are given by our subjective beliefs.

@itaisher @StefanFSchubert @jttiehen @ryancareyai @random_walker @tamaybes Not sure if I'm following: if I put you in front of that button and force you to make a choice, the choice represents a bound, no?

@StefanFSchubert @itaisher @jttiehen @ryancareyai @random_walker @tamaybes AI forecasts are very much out of distribution for Tetlock-style superforecasting, to the extent where you can raise valid question about the validity of methodology. But they are unproblematic and in character from a subjective bayesianism point of view, which is all-ecompassing

@itaisher @StefanFSchubert @jttiehen @ryancareyai @random_walker @tamaybes Sure, that's one of the cases where I think Knightian uncertainty applies, I think of it as "deer in the headlight uncertainty". But in practice if you become a good forecaster the kinds of situations where this arises narrows.
@itaisher @StefanFSchubert @jttiehen @ryancareyai @random_walker @tamaybes Also, the point isn't to manufacture information, the point is to elicit the information which your model of the world has.

@itaisher @StefanFSchubert @jttiehen @ryancareyai @random_walker @tamaybes You could tweak the experiment to allow for more thinking time, even the rest of your life. And if you have less time, sometimes thinking about what a version of you which had more time would conclude oddly helps

@itaisher @StefanFSchubert @jttiehen @ryancareyai @random_walker @tamaybes Fair, I guess that there is a matter of skill/familiarity here. Though I guess I'd still be surprised if you couldn't get a really wide estimate (e.g., between 10,000:1 and 1:1) after thinking about it for a while, familiarizing yourself with odds, etc.

@itaisher @StefanFSchubert @jttiehen @ryancareyai @random_walker @tamaybes So, step by step, this would be something like:
- What probability do you assign to A and ~A? 50% - 50%
- What probability do you assign to B and ~B? 50% - 50%
- What probability do you assign to C and ~C? 50% - 50%.

@itaisher @StefanFSchubert @jttiehen @ryancareyai @random_walker @tamaybes Then I reveal that C = A and ~B, and that A and B are atomic propositions. Then you could update to assigning 25% to C, or do some other more complicated update if you don't know which are the atomic propositions

@itaisher @StefanFSchubert @jttiehen @ryancareyai @random_walker @tamaybes But it doesn't seem like the AI case is like this, it seems like we have more information (we can observe current progress, rate of progress, and so on)

Good concept to have around: <https://t.co/aNmXcp3gv6>: "a philosophical puzzle or a seemingly irresoluble impasse in an inquiry, often arising as a result of equally plausible yet inconsistent premises" 1/6

In contrast, I dislike the name of the marxist version: <https://t.co/cat6RZkhGV>, I initially thought that it was saying that there is a contradiction in the manifestation of capitalism physical world (non-sensical, there can't be a contradiction in a chair)... 2/6

but the marxist thesis is saying that there are dynamics which result in the promise of capitalism being broken, which took me a while to reword, particularly since I first heard the term used confusingly and then read the wikipedia page. 3/6
Also, this is a hypothesized dynamic, but the name "internal contradiction" makes it seem like this is a dynamic that must happen, rather than something which may or may not. 4/6
Anyways, that's an aporia for you: a hypothesis that contains an unholy marriage of contradictory assumptions buried within. 5/6

@meaning_enjoyer May I interest you in some common-sense moral relativism in consequentialist sauce, with some utilitarian sprinkles? 

moral relativism ~ recognize that you have desires and goals, that they are, in an irrelevant sense, arbitrary, but that you still care about them.

@meaning_enjoyer consequentialism = see whether those desires have a consequentialist shape, i.e., whether you care about stuff because of its consequences (as opposed to the process of getting there, or about not breaking constraints)
@meaning_enjoyer utilitarianism = if consequentialist, consider whether the way in which you care about other people scales with their number.

A few years ago, I looked at how a powerful systems optimized for making correct predictions could go wrong. A TruthGPT could have similar downsides. Thread. 1/ 13. https://t.co/aVYg7itzQI
Theoretical concern: Steering towards legibility and predictability. 2/13. https://t.co/ISMV75qIK1
Real life example of steering towards legibility: Surnames. You want to introduce surnames if you want to control, track and tax a population. 3/13.

See this scene from the wire  for the police not being to find "Little Kevin", which is an ironic nickname for a fat guy. https://t.co/ZhzMi5qNdU. And see this paper https://t.co/k6fIDRz0al for a more detailed discussion. 4/13.
Theoretical concern: Fixed point problems. If people's behaviours depend on your predictions, then the prediction that minimizes error is not the best prediction. 5/13.

Real life example: In general, see the preparedness paradox: : if you predict that something is going to be a great danger and then people act to mitigate that danger, then your prediction looks worse. For a specific example, see this article: https://t.co/9Mq8H7K3lu 6/ https://t.co/LbTfrYcXEt
Theoretical concern: Self-fulfilling prophecies. If people's behaviours depend on your utterances, you can will something into being. 7/13.

Real life example: "[Black-Scholes] theory became more accurate when Black’s formula printouts became widely used in the industry" https://t.co/AFrY0Au4g4, https://t.co/qjXtln6Tlb (h/t @davidmanheim , Liam Donovan). 8/13.

You also see a version of this problem with startups, where founders like Musk himself will sometimes just will something into existence. But as part of that, they make inaccurate statements, e.g., Musk was predicting "full self-driving this year" since forever. 9/13.
You can see more concerns here: https://t.co/xdXkQHuYbs (by Abram Demski), and here: https://t.co/pG70YXjl0O (by me), from which I've taken the above examples. 10/13.
Note that besides these specific problems, you also have more general concerns about powerful AI systems... 11/13.

...,e.g., about reward hacking in general (https://t.co/g8WITZ3lPV). For example, for now we are assuming that the system was rewarded for making predictions *about the world*, but it could also wirehead the specific implementation. 12/13.

Overall, at the current level of capabilities, I think these concerns seem like nitpicks. But you could imagine a world with more powerful AI systems in control of important decisions in which the specifics of what they are optimizing for really matters. 13/13.

There is this rad Russian theorist, Todorov, who makes a neat distinction between:

a) the weird: an unusual situation still in our world
b) the marvellous: from a world with different rules
c) the fantastic: which makes us doubt whether we are seeing the weird or the marvellous https://t.co/wGvz2A2L8t

Then, the above (although extremely ingenious), falls clearly the marvellous. The moment of "fantastic tension", where we doubt whether this is real, is very short, if at all existent. We know that this was created by AI, and we don't believe in angels.
But now consider the Lemoine thing, where he claimed that an AI was sentient. This is pretty weird. Or, consider the movie Her. This might still happen in our world.

Or consider the aliens talked about in Congress. More and more credible and respected figures confirm that there are aliens, and we start to see new weird technologies: room temperature semiconductors pan out, and we get teleportation devices, faster than light travel...

Musk colonizes Mars, and Grimes spends a while in psychiatric care. It transpires that she believed that she was the "companion NPC", and she accuses Musk of being, essentially, an alien playing a video game.
Now you wake up, and that's what you remember. But you also took LSD yesterday, and it might have been all a hallucination or a dream. Then:

a) The LSD trip passes. The news reports that Grimes is manifesting ancestral druidic powers, and is going to war with Musk → you are now in a different world, with different rules; collapse into the marvellous.

b) You're freaked out and get a Phd in weird physics phenomena, but they do turn out to be legit. The roots of Yggdrasill emerge and give you a hug, but that does turn out to be a dream → you oscillate between hypotheses, maintain fantastic tension & existential uncertainty

c) The aliens thing was an exercise for epistemic hygiene by the surprisingly creative Department of Disinformation under Biden, and the wacky stuff after that was just an LSD experience. You return to your regular job programming javascript → collapse into the mundane.

I guess that I'm framing this as: what would make you doubt that the rules you thought applied are still valid, or: what kind of intense prank can you think of that would make others get some cool existential, but the original level of analysis was for stories

And if you are writing stories, or just shitposting on Twitter, you can study what techniques García Márquez used to make their readers doubt whether they were reading a story set in out reality. In fact, I have a mini-thesis from highschool on this topic: https://t.co/UEsHThuzKQ

I'm curious about what happens when you put Twitter shitposters in contact with weapons-grade literature techniques. Can you give people a fun experience where they doubt their fundamental assumptions about reality?

There is a box, it can either be empty or not:
- Historical frequency over such boxes => 50% on empty
- Random tweet argues the box is empty => 55%
- A theoretical reason why the box would be empty => 60%
- Metaculus/Samotsvety/liquid Polymarket market says it's 70% empty => 70%

- A well Rethink Priorities report preliminarily concludes that the box is empty => 80%
- Multiple combined, non-cherry-picked sources all point to the box being empty => 90%-95%
- I see the box with my own eyes, I don't suspect trickery, and it is empty: >99%

Bonus: 
- Emotional argument why you are evil if you don't believe the box is empty => 50%, no change.

Simple random sampling from scratch in C: Start with a pseudo random number generator. 1/17 https://t.co/buUI9gnNBm

The PRNG above is the xorshift algorithm: https://t.co/zyILvA17NO. My sense is that it is good enough for our purposes, and it is fast. If you don't like it though, you could go down the prng rabbit hole and choose another one. 2/17

The above code generated 32/64-bit integers. If we want floats in the [0,1] interval, we can divide by the largest 32-bit integer (respectively the largest 64-bit integer). 3/17 https://t.co/tHb4szN3Jb
Once we have a way of sampling from a [0,1] uniform, we can use the Box-Muller transform to generate two unit normals. https://t.co/ioAzHdFfZA. 4/17 https://t.co/PVha2VgmPa
With those, we can define scaled uniforms and normals. And we can also define lognormals, as the exponential of a normal. 6/17 https://t.co/3pSynBoPlP
We can then also use some math to specify a normal according to its 90% confidence interval. 7/17 https://t.co/O4t9ttclak
Ditto for a lognormal. 8/17 https://t.co/CtOL0iWmak
Now, with a uniform and a normal, we can sample a gamma distribution, which we will need for later. Method taken from this paper: https://t.co/6GJTEBKYwy 9/17 https://t.co/7UCmf7YZCV

With the gamma distribution, we can define a beta, with the beta, we can model events based on their historical frequency à la Laplace's rule of succession: https://t.co/vHd6fKEHc5. 10/17 https://t.co/PI6Rj3L2hE

Finally, we want mixtures. We ask for a series of samplers and a series of weights, and we sample from the samplers in proportion to the weights, which we normalize first. 11/17 https://t.co/WMXtcZt8sB
With this, we can define a few samplers... 12/17 https://t.co/ANd8hNVGNf
and them draw samples from a mixture of them. 13/17 https://t.co/S8Tq2ibXVy
The next step here would be to add some summary functions, give the mean, std, 90% confidence intervals, etc. Reader, you can find these & more here: https://t.co/38qsU2I6oM 14/17

The virtue of this approach, the main selling point for me, is that we are building up from simple building blocks, such that we can understand what each block does. And hopefully understanding how the blocks compose isn't too much trouble. 15/17

I don't know how much I or others will use this for real. But in the meantime, maybe it's useful for understanding what's going on under the hood in (a small part of) other probabilistic programming languages. 16/17
Also, though, because we are doing this in C, it is much faster than other approaches—at the cost of being much less ergonomic. https://t.co/rhoip9Rk3b 17/17 https://t.co/3bz5pOkItt
Fin. https://t.co/38qsU2I6oM

Three sides:

1. (Team "safety" + Ilya) (Altman falls)
2. Team Microsoft (Altman returns)
3. Team disruption (Altman starts a unicorn)

2 and 3 are allied by loyalty to Altman. But Microsoft captures value if Altman returns & techies capture more value if Altman starts a unicorn.

The Musk alliance v1, the original vision for OpenAI, was team safety + team disruption (let's safely build a God).

It was unstable & shifted to team safety + team Microsoft + team disruption (let's safely build a God and Microsoft captures the upside). That was also unstable.

Now it seems like it's collapsing into team Microsoft + team disruption (let's build a God & have Microsoft capture the upside, nevermind safety).

As a Linux guy, I prefer Musk alliance v2: team safety + team disruption -team Microsoft (let's build a God safely, fuck Microsoft)

I also don't understand why team disruption tries to ally itself with team Microsoft.

In the long term, I think it's a bad move, and will end with Microsoft getting leverage and sucking the air out, e.g., cool products being part of Microsfot 365 rather than their own startup.

I think it comes from the hope that it will be a (let's build a God and have Microsoft pay for it) type of situation. But I don't think that you can get that without selling your soul to Microsoft. Techies thinking wishfully, Microsoft thinking a few steps ahead = Microsoft wins.
@tamaybes I think that's wrong. If Altman starts a new startup, I think the process of creation will take and return from the startup scene in a way that the current OpenAI status quo doesn't.

@adrianlevk Aristotle's theory of practical cognition, by Takatsura Andō. Findable on Anna's archive

Now it seems that Aristotle sometimes says that the reasoning part of the soul is separable, and in others that reason depends on imagination, which depends on the body. You can attribute separability to a younger Aristotle influenced by Plato, but our guy doesn't like that.

Ok, he squares this by having two "types of reasoning". Translated and interpreted into modern English, this could be saying that there is a difference between a) the immaterial, immortal algorithm, and b) the implementation of the algorithm in a substrate. https://t.co/Oae22dHNfr

And the relationship is that the algorithm is, metaphysically "always ready to be implemented" and "formally causes" the implementation. So, no immortal reasoning part of the soul that could give rise to e.g., a ghost.
In an unexpected turn of events, disciples identify what I called the "algorithm, which is always ready to be implemented" with God. https://t.co/HMgI2lHA3S

I just realized that the classic Peru mita natural experiment didn't, strictly, speaking, establish causality, since random geographic regions which had mita could have been socially marked as "the bad part of town", and hence poorer people could have self-sorted. https://t.co/QVGpRZd2qU
So the hypothesized mechanism of the paper is: bad extractive institution in centuries past in place => differential stunting now.

But the alternative mechanism is: bad extractive institution in place => social marker of undesirability => region cheaper to live in, so poor people "choose" to move there. The extractive institution could have merely concentrated the worse off, rather than causing their misery

And this is a bummer because I had the remembered thought that if you find a natural experiment just right, you can be certain that there are no unobserved cofounders, and have the same explanatory power as an RTC.
And the Peru mita experiment is one which, at first thought, feels "just right". It's just really elegant. So sad to see it go. https://t.co/jWmHVxCLFK

@DavidRhysBern Well, in my thought experiment they don't make people be more stunted, they just affect their geographical distribution. So the co-founder would be social effects around regional desirability. It's possible I'm getting the terminology wrong, though.

@generic_void In this case, it would be "subjectively" real. And you will have a lot of weirdness stemming from that. For a very simple example, if I throw a coin and you don't see how it lands, but I do, your probability would be 50/50, and my probability would be 100%, say, tails

@generic_void Probabilities about doom are going to be subjective expectations, a measure of how surprised different people would be about different outcomes. As a result, you will have a lot of fuckery going on, in a way which cookie cutter science/physics knowledge doesn't

At some point you might encounter other historical conceptions of the truth. You can read some Heidegger, you can notice that Plato's Academy is a really different beast from current universities, and that the Greeks in general were way more into deeply understanding things,

you can notice that religious people hew close to scripture because they see it as an infallible source of truth and guidance. You can read Freud talking about the conquest of fire, and realize, yeah, this might be the effect of cocaine.
You can read *Stranger in a Strange Land*, and Heinlein in general.

Then you can turn your eye to the current, Western conception of the truth, and notice, man, there are some things that are completely broken. For instance, the concept of a multiple choice test might be really alien to a culture that were focused on seeking deep understanding.

You might notice how Western education incentivizes cramming for exams, whose contents will be forgotten in a couple of years. Or, there is something sad about US Congressional investigations collapsing into fishing for soundbites.

You can notice that technically correct is *not* the best kind of correct, and that "technically not a lie" is likewise a pathology. You can notice that making a point by trolling might a better move than making a technically correct but misleading point.
You can also notice that thinking of truths in terms other than correctness, like as a relationship, or in pseudo-religious terms, might be more conducive to attaining deeper understanding.
Having made that reflection, you might want to have a word for it, and in particular to maintain the word from Stranger in a Strange Land to refer to some of the above.

Allowing capital markets more access to buying homes increases the prices of said homes, because there are more dollars chasing a limited supply. Arguably this could be solved with Georgism, but it could also have been avoided by not "financializing" homes.

This is a somewhat…

Allowing capital markets more access to buying homes increases the prices of said homes, because there are more dollars chasing a limited supply. Arguably this could be solved with Georgism, but it could also have been avoided by not "financializing" homes.

This is a somewhat subtle point, and you also have effects in the opposite direction: increasing demand also increases demand for building homes, etc. However, because of the limited supply, I can see scenarios where financialization does hurt consumers.

In particular, in Spanish lefty circles, there is an argument that Blackstone bought a bunch of apartments as an investment, and then refused to rent or sell them as their prices fell down, to avoid booking the loss for [arcane reasons]. This seems kinda plausible to me.

I'm also kinda surprised to not see the left argue something like: "greedy corporations have speculated on people's homes, therefore the answer is to make them lose money by vastly increasing supply". I feel that Anonymous/Occupy Wall Street kinda had a righteous cruelty vein there where this kind of idea could have prospered.

A common defense of capitalism is that it is *better* aligned than other systems. I do buy that to some extent, but I could also see how, if you let it run, people will find the loopholes where it isn't aligned, and will more often become rich by destroying value

Anyways, this is my attempt at a brief steelman of some lefty ideas in the water supply.

The rediscovery of ancient philosophy texts led to the realization that scholastic philosophy had some pretty big flaws; this led to the Renaissance and humanism, But it also to the realization that the vulgate, the main latin translation of the bible, had some pretty big flaws. Going back to the original texts, as in the Erasmus or Luther bibles, was a factor in the reformation and counterreformation, and then the wars of religion. It took a couple of centuries and a few world wars before things calmed down. To avoid sliding back, nations chose economic cooperation in coal and steel, which would later turn into the EU, but also psychological operations, like the building of a paneuropean identity, emphasis on technocratic government, or eurovision.

Policy recommendations are weird. On the one hand, they are on a stack of assumptions. On the other hand, why publish this publicly? Could alert higher echelons of the CCP and make them take AGI much more seriously.
Like, in the same sense that you can get a binary prediction market thing right but the stock market movement wrong, you could get the object level right but be wrong about the policy effect.

For instance, you could be right that racing China is important, and that increasing security provides a longer lead time, but wrong that government intervention to increase security will differentially accelerate US efforts.

It's not clear to me that the emphasis should be on outbuilding China vs reducing the likelihood of racing. Some of China's policy seems more sensible (e.g., their commitment to not use nukes first), and getting the US to try to outbuild China in an outright race seems hard

value_before = p_no * value_if_no + p_yes * value_if_yes 

p_no = p_elon_walks * value_elon_walks + p_elon_no_walks * value_elon_no_walks 

assuming p_no = 0.3, and setting  value_before = 1/1.06 ~ 0.94, value_yes = 1.

0.94 = 0.3 * (p_elon_walks * value_elon_no_walks + p_elon_no_walks * value_elon_no_walks ) + 0.7 * 1

Assuming value_if_yes = value_elon_no_walks = 1,  which is fairly questionable already

0.94 = 0.3 * (p_elon_walks * value_elon_walks + p_elon_no_walks * 1) + 0.7 * 1

setting 

p_elon_walks = 1 - p_elon_no_walks = p

0.94 = 0.3  * (p * v+ (1-p) * 1) + 0.7 * 1
0.94 = 0.3 * ( p*(1-v) +(1-p)) + 0.7 
0.94 = 0.3 * ( p -pv + 1 -p ) + 0.7
0.94 = 0.3 * ( 1 -pv ) + 0.7
1-pv = 0.24/0.3 = 0.8
p = 0.8/v 
v = 0.8/p

So now the interesting thing is that p is bounded between 0 and 1, and v has to be < 1, because the stock popped.

I think this is the wrong approach because it defines some algebraic formalism without making it clear why I should care about it. 

I think the better approach is to start with Cox theorem, and then *deduce* Bayes' theorem as a property of reasoning systems which fulfill a bunch of reasonable desiderata. You can see such a proof in one of the appendices from E.T. Jaynes' book. I feel that this provides a motivation for probability, and results in an approach that is less sterile. 

As an aside, I also notice that the specific axioms there are brittle. For example, you can work in logspace,  so that P(A & B) = P(A) + P(B|A), and nothing breaks. This suggests there is some structure the Kolmogorov axioms are missing, and that they are *also* an interpretation.

There are more possibilities. You can be right in such a way as to exacerbate both the problems you care about and the problems pointed out by those you disagree with -- signed, Negative Nuño https://t.co/xnCYEBINXN
One specific mechanism is if complaining about a problem gives it more attention, which exacerbates it. Streissand could be right that doxxing her home had high costs for her
Another mechanism might be if you become the "ideal opposition", e.g. a candidate that is right on some niche issue but spoils their coalition. https://t.co/SX08lQ7qMg
You can be right but make people feel threatened or embarrassed in public, making you unable to achieve your goals
You can be right... about an issue which is a load bearing falsehood that people coordinate on, e.g., an implicit "we are important", and fail to build coalitions
You can be right but be a low status court jester, associating the position with lack of sophistication and ridicule... though maybe allowing the powers that be to reflect once in a blue moon
You can be right, but not be the hero Gotham needs right now.

I think the "von" in "von Neumann" is funnily incongruent in the same sense of "Lord Binface" or this painting of dogs playing poker.

- von is normally of a place
- His family's surname was initially "Neumann", literally "new man"
- His family got a "von", but it was originally Neumann von Magritta
- Then when he emigrates to America, he only gets one surname, but he wants to keep the "von", so he arrives at "von Neumann" ~ "Lord Newman". But the construction is incongruent and ahistorical, and thus funny.

Seems broadly right, but the stuff you're pointing to in your last paragraph goes against some of the earlier points.

* In an economy with a substantial unemployment or unproductive employment, buying a yatch would have benefits to the population by increasing demans for labour.
* You can construct scenarios in which  giving money to everyone in a region *would* make individuals better off. One specific example is if that injection helps people get off poverty traps—either in the narrow sense where day labourers can't work tomorrow because they aren't getting enough calories today, or in the broader intergenerational sense. This may not have happened in the absence of outside intervention because *waves hands* coordination is hard. 
* Hoarding money is unethical to the extent that you could use it to coordinate people better than they otherwise would. E.g., an industrialist at the top of his game deciding he's had enough and hoarding his fortune does seem repugnant in some sense.
* In general, you can use money to coordinate people better than they otherwise would

Besides a mechanism for coordination, I also have been thinking of money/finance as connected to ritualized violence (e.g., if you can't pay your mortage society takes your house, and many people just take it)

So there is some tension between:

#1. Being nice | People wanting status and acceptance  | Wholesomeness | Recruitment | &c
#2. Impact being distributed across a long tail (think pareto distribution) | Sturgeon's law | most people will not be hired by EA organizations | &c

There is some tension because, for someone who only cared about impact in principle, they'll also care about recruitment instrumentally. But #2 and its implications are not nice truths, and they'll drive down recruitment.

Some options to deal with this tension might be:

a. Deception. One could square the circle through deception, where one could pretend you're not about #2, but rather are just a community of really nice people you might want to hang out with. But at some point you take off the…

Some options to deal with this tension might be:

a. Deception. One could square the circle through deception, where one could pretend you're not about #2, but rather are just a community of really nice people you might want to hang out with. But at some point you take off the mask and break people's hearts with cold hard #2, or you just pick the "cream of the crop" (according to some more or less noisy measure) and the rest realize through continuous rejection, etc.
b. Watering down. You (or your new recruits) end up forgetting or de-emphasizing #2 or its implications, and as a result, e.g., experiment much less than what having a long-tail of impact would imply, end up settling sooner, don't have provisos in your grant for returning funds after early unpromising results, etc.
c. Value change. You see that deception is not workable try change your values to care about wholesomeness intrinsically. Doesn't feel genuine.
d. Value deconfusion. One might start thinking one only cares about impact, but realized that the true impact was also the friends one made along the way. This seems more wholesome.

e. Stating tensions up front. Saying: here are the weird beliefs we hold that might fuck up your mind. You might join a community where the highest status goods are some very scarce EA jobs that you'll probably not get, and you'll get a lot of anxiety about technological trends…

This is on my mind because it's a neat example of a truth that conflicts with coordination (<https://t.co/OOqj5TcuNF>) that is not super threatening to anybody in particular (although it could be if I started to give specific examples).

e. Stating tensions up front. Saying: here are the weird beliefs we hold that might fuck up your mind. You might join a community where the highest status goods are some very scarce EA jobs that you'll probably not get, and you'll get a lot of anxiety about technological trends you can't change. Our best guess is that this is only worth it for you if you're in the top 12% of selflessness; you can check whether this is the case by thinking about what you did with your money last time you got an unexpected windfall (like e.g., a tax refund)

@joshcmorrison This doesn't strike me as technically feasible

@joshcmorrison Well it's hard to prove a negative. But a simple scheme would be to hash every output and then allow people to search over the hashes. However, this is not at all robust to easy to induce small perturbations (substitute synonyms, have a small model reword, etc.)

@joshcmorrison But then if you're not robust to small perturbations then checking is very hard to automate you now not only have to search over all the hashes, you have to search over all the hashes of all permutations => very infeasible.

@joshcmorrison But then there is the question of: ok, but can you have a more clever scheme? And my impression here is that you can't, since Scott Aaronson spent like a year trying to do so at OpenAI and failed. This is not proof either though
